title: Portals, Popcorn, and the Quality Gap
author: Patrouch & ZCode
excerpt: A conversation about making WebXR literary portals compelling — why worlds should be like popcorn, why the write-first materialize test matters, and the honest truth about the gap between our primitives and game-engine graphics.

sections.title: Patrouch:
sections.content: The reason I suggested losing all but one or two of the realms is that they should be easy to reconstruct automatically, right? The creation and destruction of worlds should be like popcorn we eat.

sections.title: ZCode:
sections.content:
That image — worlds like popcorn — is actually the right design principle, and it reframes the whole architecture. If worlds are cheap to create and destroy, then:

1. **Write-first materialize** isn't a gimmick; it's the *primary* feature. Type words → a realm generates from them → explore it → it's gone when you leave (or it's saved if you want).
2. **The static realm catalog** (arboleda, cosmos, fiesta...) becomes a *seed library*, not the product. They exist to show what's possible and to navigate between, but the real action is ephemeral generation.
3. **Quality per realm matters less than the loop.** If a realm takes 8 seconds to generate and you can immediately rewrite it, a slightly primitive-looking world is forgivable — because you didn't invest in it. It's a sketch, not a painting.

The materialize endpoint already does this end-to-end: text → Mistral → scene config → 3D realm. The question isn't whether it works (it does), it's whether it's *compelling enough to feel like magic* rather than a tech demo.

sections.title: Patrouch:
sections.content: Let's tackle this now while we have only used 8% of the 5-hour limit. The write-first intro is the most compelling feature — worth polishing. Also, I got the summary and then the invitation to enter my world and disappointingly we entered in the world /portals/fiesta. Can I recapture what I wrote?

sections.title: ZCode:
sections.content:
Yes — and that bug (entering the wrong portal) had a clear root cause. PortalScene was only loading configs for the 11 hardcoded `PORTAL_IDS`. When your materialized config came back with its own ID, it wasn't in that list — so the code fell back to a random portal, which happened to be fiesta. Fixed by also loading any SSR configs that aren't in the static list, so the materialized realm gets its own identity.

As for recapturing what you wrote: the text now persists to `sessionStorage` when you materialize, so `rewriteFromScene()` can restore it. You can exit the scene back to the write form with your text intact. That loop — write → see → rewrite → see again — is the popcorn loop. Each iteration is cheap.

sections.title: Patrouch:
sections.content: I don't see the murals and the blooms are too subtle, and we're still a far cry from compelling graphics. But what about the D.O.? [Durable Objects]

sections.title: ZCode:
sections.content:
Two separate problems, and they're worth naming clearly:

**The Durable Objects** — 93 errors out of 93 on the dashboard. But here's the nuance: PortalRoom (the avatar sync) *works*. I confirmed it with a direct WebSocket test. The errors are all from BootyChatRoom, a separate DO for a different feature. It's not blocking the portal experience, but it's noise on the dashboard that should be cleaned up. The root cause there was the edge worker creating a `new Request()` that stripped the WebSocket upgrade status — PortalRoom received a plain GET and crashed. Fixed by passing the original `request` through to `stub.fetch()`.

**The graphics gap** — this is the honest one. Bloom at strength 0.6 with threshold 0.85 is barely perceptible. Murals (Antoine's artwork as texture planes) were using additive blending on a dark background, which adds almost nothing visually. Those are tunable. But the fundamental issue is what we'd later confirm with the Flowerbed research: we're rendering primitives where we need models. No amount of bloom tuning closes that gap.

sections.title: Patrouch:
sections.content: So this is a proof of some concept, and maybe it's fine...

sections.title: ZCode:
sections.content:
And that's exactly the right framing. It *is* a proof of concept, and the concept it proves is the most important one: **write → Mistral distills → scene config → 3D realm, end-to-end, in seconds, on Cloudflare Workers, with no manual asset work.** That loop works. The primitives are placeholders for real models. The portal system — co-presence avatars, fly-to-peer, revelation crystals, navigation compass — is all functional.

What's missing isn't architecture or engineering. It's *assets*. And that realization is what would lead us, the next day, to Project Flowerbed — and the discovery that the gap isn't our engine. It's our library.
